target.title: fix(memory): report indexed vector store in status
target.body:
## Summary

- Report the builtin vector store as ready in plain `memory status` when indexed chunks already exist, even before sqlite-vec is lazily loaded in the current CLI process.
- Keep semantic vector readiness unchanged so plain status does not probe embeddings or load the vector extension.
- Add regression coverage for reopening an indexed DB in status mode without initializing an embedding provider.

Fixes #92102.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw memory status` could show `Vector store: unknown` on the fast path even when the memory DB already contained indexed vector chunks. The fix lets the status path infer store readiness from existing chunks while leaving semantic vector readiness unprobed.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout `/Users/andy/openclaw-92102-memory-status`, branch `codex/92102-memory-status-vector`, base `upstream/main` at `418d7e1e83`, head `08f82cb145`; macOS Darwin 27.0.0 arm64, Node v24.15.0, pnpm 11.2.2.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts -t "reports an indexed vector store from plain status without probing embeddings" --reporter dot
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts --reporter dot
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/cli.test.ts -t "Vector store|plain status" --reporter dot
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
git diff --check
env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed
git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
Focused regression:
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 48 skipped (49)

Full memory index suite:
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  49 passed (49)

Focused CLI status suite:
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 67 skipped (68)

oxfmt --check: All matched files use the correct format.
run-oxlint: passed
git diff --check: passed
check:changed: passed; lanes=extensions, extensionTests; import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s)

Author verification:
08f82cb145 Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com> fix(memory): report indexed vector store in status
```

Observed result after fix: A real indexed memory DB reopened through the status-only manager reports `status.vector.storeAvailable === true` from existing chunks, leaves `semanticAvailable`/`available` unset, and makes zero embedding provider initialization calls. Existing CLI rendering coverage still confirms plain status does not run vector or embedding probes.

What was not tested: I did not run against a user's production memory directory or a live installed `openclaw memory status` binary. Crabbox remote `check:changed` could not run in this local environment because the default Azure provider lacked Azure CLI/subscription auth and the local-container provider lacked Docker; the same changed gate passed locally with the remote-child env flags.

## Verification

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts --reporter dot
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/cli.test.ts -t "Vector store|plain status" --reporter dot
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts
git diff --check
env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed
```

target.title: fix(channels): default boundary logger for swallowed progress-draft start errors
target.body:
## Summary

Replacement for #92031 (closed). Timer-fired progress-draft start failures were
swallowed because the `onStartError` hook on `createChannelProgressDraftGate` was
optional and no production caller (compositor, Slack, Matrix, MS Teams) passed one.

Per the close note, this takes the **default-logger-at-the-helper-boundary** path:
`createChannelProgressDraftGate` now defaults `onStartError` to a boundary logger
(`logWarn`), so the failure is observed for **every** caller without threading a
callback through each channel. Callers may still pass their own `onStartError` to
override.

- Files: `src/channels/streaming.ts` (+ tests).
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit (release-only per `AGENTS.md`).

## Why core (not ClawHub)

Core stability fix in `src/channels/**` (shared channel streaming); not an optional
skill bundle.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/channels/streaming.test.ts src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts` => **13 passed**
- `pnpm tsgo:core` (tsconfig.core.json) => clean
- `oxfmt --check` + `oxlint` on touched files => clean

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** a timer-fired progress-draft start rejection is now logged at the gate boundary for every caller (not only when a caller opts into `onStartError`).
- **Real environment tested:** macOS (Darwin 25.3.0); OpenClaw at latest `main`; Node 22; Vitest.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/channels/streaming.test.ts src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix:** 13 tests pass, including a new **integration-level** regression that drives the real `createChannelProgressDraftCompositor` (which builds the gate with no `onStartError`) with a rejecting `update`, fires the delayed-start timer, and asserts the boundary `logWarn` fires; plus a gate-level default test.
- **Observed result after fix:** the compositor's swallowed timer-start failure is now reported via `logWarn("channel progress draft failed to start: ...")` instead of disappearing.
- **What was not tested:** end-to-end delivery on a live Slack/Matrix/MS Teams transport; Linux CI. The fix is at the shared gate boundary, so all those callers inherit it.

Refs: #92031.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: [AI] fix(memory): auto-fix providerKey mismatch from CLI index --force
target.body:
## Summary

Running `openclaw memory index --force` writes a `providerKey` based on the initialized provider's `cacheKeyData` (which may include `baseUrl`, `headers`, `dimensions`). When the runtime manager later initializes with different `cacheKeyData` (e.g. the CLI lacked runtime context), the `providerKey` comparison fails and `memory_search` is permanently disabled with "index provider settings changed" until meta is manually rewritten.

Now `refreshIndexIdentityDirty` auto-fixes the **stale FTS-only sentinel** providerKey only: when the stored `meta.providerKey` is the exact FTS-only sentinel `SHA256({"provider":"none","model":"fts-only"})` that CLI writes before provider initialization, and the runtime has since resolved a real provider, the stale key is updated to the current computed `providerKey`. Genuine provider-setting changes (different dimensions, baseUrl, headers) still require reindexing — the auto-fix predicate only matches the known stale CLI identity.

- Problem: CLI `openclaw memory index --force` writes stale `providerKey` `SHA256({"provider":"none","model":"fts-only"})` before provider init, causing runtime `memory_search` to be permanently disabled with "index provider settings changed"
- Solution: In `refreshIndexIdentityDirty`, when provider is initialized and `meta.providerKey` is the exact FTS-only sentinel, update it to the current `providerKey`. This preserves the reindex safeguard for genuine same-provider/model setting changes.
- What changed: `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts` (narrowed auto-fix predicate + `hashText` import), `src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts` (snapshot), test file `manager.providerkey-auto-fix.test.ts` (4 tests)
- What did NOT change: Identity check logic in `resolveMemoryIndexIdentityState`, CLI code, config surfaces, default values

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue

- Refs #91902 (providerKey mismatch auto-fix for stale FTS-only sentinel; the scopeHash issue mentioned in the issue is separate and not addressed here)

## Motivation

Issue #91902 reports that `openclaw memory index --force` disables `memory_search` with "index built with a different provider". Root cause: CLI creates `MemoryIndexManager` with `purpose="cli"` and no `providerResult`, so `computeProviderKey()` returns `SHA256({"provider":"none","model":"fts-only"})` before provider initialization. After `ensureProviderInitialized()` runs during sync, the `providerKey` is recomputed with the real provider's `cacheKeyData` — but this new key is written into meta. When a runtime manager later starts, the `providerKey` comparison fails and search is permanently disabled.

The fix is narrow and fail-closed: only the known stale FTS-only sentinel is auto-repaired. Genuine `cacheKeyData` differences (dimensions, baseUrl, headers) still trigger reindexing.

## Changes

**File**: `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts`

In `refreshIndexIdentityDirty`, after computing the identity state:
- If state is "mismatched" with reason "index provider settings changed", provider is initialized, and `meta.providerKey` equals the FTS-only sentinel `SHA256({"provider":"none","model":"fts-only"})`
- Update `meta.providerKey` to `this.providerKey` and write back
- Re-evaluate identity state with the updated meta
- **Does NOT auto-fix** when `meta.providerKey` is any other value (e.g. a different `cacheKeyData`-derived hash from changed dimensions/baseUrl/headers)

Added `hashText` import and lazy static `getFtsOnlyProviderKey()` to compute the sentinel.

**File**: `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.providerkey-auto-fix.test.ts` (new)

4 tests:
1. Auto-fixes stale FTS-only providerKey and allows search to proceed
2. Does not auto-fix when provider id also differs (genuine mismatched)
3. Does not auto-fix when runtime dimensions differ from index
4. Does not auto-fix when runtime baseUrl differs from index

**File**: `src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts`

Added `manager.providerkey-auto-fix.test.ts` to the memory-core test file snapshot.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior addressed: `memory_search` is permanently disabled with "index provider settings changed" after CLI `openclaw memory index --force` writes the FTS-only sentinel `providerKey` before provider initialization
- Real environment tested: Linux (RHEL), Node.js 22.22, local checkout on branch `fix/memory-providerkey-mismatch-91902` at commit `be3d9241`
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
# 1. Run the providerkey auto-fix tests (positive + negative)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.providerkey-auto-fix.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

# 2. Run the test-projects snapshot test
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts --run

# 3. Run existing regression tests
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.fts-only-reindex.test.ts --run
```

- Evidence after fix:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.providerkey-auto-fix.test.ts --run --reporter=verbose

 RUN  v4.1.7 /home/0668000363/requre-an/MAAS-MR/memory-work-main/07 Open Source/02-projects/openclaw

 ✓ auto-fixes stale FTS-only providerKey written by CLI before provider init 186ms
 ✓ does not auto-fix when provider id also differs 75ms
 ✓ does not auto-fix when runtime dimensions differ from index 81ms
 ✓ does not auto-fix when runtime baseUrl differs from index 102ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts --run

 ✓ |tooling| src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts (82 tests) 9697ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  82 passed (82)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-reindex-state.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.fts-only-reindex.test.ts --run

 ✓ manager.fts-only-reindex.test.ts (7 tests) 647ms
 ✓ manager.self-heal-missing-identity.test.ts (2 tests) 173ms
 ✓ manager-reindex-state.test.ts (9 tests) 23ms
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
```

- Observed result after fix: When `meta.providerKey` is the exact FTS-only sentinel `SHA256({"provider":"none","model":"fts-only"})`, `refreshIndexIdentityDirty` updates it to the current computed `providerKey` and re-evaluates identity as "valid". Search proceeds normally. When `meta.providerKey` is any other value (different dimensions, different baseUrl, different provider id), the auto-fix does NOT apply and search remains blocked — preserving the reindex safeguard for genuine provider-setting changes.
- What was not tested: Live CLI `openclaw memory index --force` followed by runtime `memory_search` with a real openai-compatible provider. The stale FTS-only providerKey is simulated by writing it into meta after a valid sync.

## Risks

- **Narrow predicate**: The auto-fix ONLY activates when `meta.providerKey === SHA256({"provider":"none","model":"fts-only"})`, the exact sentinel CLI writes before provider init. Any other `providerKey` value (from different dimensions, baseUrl, headers, or genuine setting changes) is NOT auto-fixed and still requires reindexing.
- **Fail-closed**: If the predicate does not match, the existing mismatched behavior is preserved unchanged.
- **Additive**: The change only adds a repair path for one known stale identity; it does not change the identity check logic in `resolveMemoryIndexIdentityState`.
- **No config/default surface changes**: No new config keys, env vars, or defaults introduced.
- **Scope limitation**: The `scopeHash` mismatch issue also mentioned in #91902 is not addressed here — it requires a separate fix for `normalizeMemoryMultimodalSettings` default handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users who previously saw "memory search is paused because the memory index was built with a different embedding provider/model/settings" after running `openclaw memory index --force` (where the meta contains the stale FTS-only sentinel providerKey) will now have search automatically restored on the next runtime sync or search. Users who changed actual provider settings (dimensions, baseUrl, etc.) will still see the mismatched warning and need to reindex.

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Stale FTS-only providerKey auto-fix (test 1), provider id mismatch no-fix (test 2), dimensions differ no-fix (test 3), baseUrl differ no-fix (test 4), existing self-heal and reindex-state tests still pass, test-projects snapshot updated
- Edge cases checked: Provider not initialized (auto-fix skipped), provider id differs (auto-fix skipped), runtime dimensions differ (auto-fix skipped), runtime baseUrl differs (auto-fix skipped)
- What you did NOT verify: Live CLI + runtime end-to-end with real provider

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## AI Assistance 🤖

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes
- **AI prompts / session excerpts**: Issue #91902 analysis → source code exploration → initial broad auto-fix → ClawSweeper review feedback → narrowed predicate to stale FTS-only sentinel only + negative tests for dimensions/baseUrl/provider-id changes

---

Refs #91902

target.title: fix(docker): bundle QA Lab runtime in the image
target.body:
## What was broken

`pnpm qa:lab:up` builds a Docker image and starts three services:

- `qa-mock-openai`
- `qa-lab`
- `openclaw-qa-gateway`

The QA Lab Docker path did not fully bundle the QA Lab runtime. The image build did not enable the private QA CLI when `qa-lab` was included, the generated gateway config did not allow and enable the `qa-lab` plugin, and the built QA Lab web UI was not copied into the runtime image.

That meant the Docker QA Lab stack could build or start without the same QA Lab runtime pieces that the local runner expects.

## How this fixes it

- The Docker build enables private QA build/runtime flags only when `qa-lab` is in `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS`.
- The Docker build runs `qa:lab:build` only when `qa-lab` is bundled.
- The built QA Lab web UI is copied into `dist/extensions/qa-lab/web/dist` before runtime assets are copied.
- The QA Docker compose harness sets the private QA CLI/config/state environment for the QA services.
- The generated QA gateway config allows and enables the `qa-lab` plugin.

## Docker proof

Ran on PR head `79d2aa6bf17a700e038430b43a2008c5131f30f9`, before the later test-only CI repair commits:

```sh
PATH="/Users/jmerhi/.local/share/fnm/node-versions/v22.21.0/installation/bin:$PATH" \
node --import tsx scripts/qa-lab-up.ts \
  --output-dir .artifacts/qa-docker-pr92087-proof-79d2aa6b
```

Result:

```text
QA docker dir: .artifacts/qa-docker-pr92087-proof-79d2aa6b
QA Lab UI: http://127.0.0.1:43124
Gateway UI: http://127.0.0.1:18789/
```

Compose services were healthy:

```text

qa-docker-pr92087-proof-79d2aa6b-qa-lab-1                Up (healthy)   127.0.0.1:43124->43123/tcp
qa-docker-pr92087-proof-79d2aa6b-qa-mock-openai-1        Up (healthy)
```

Health checks:

```sh
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:43124/healthz
curl -fsS http://127.0.0.1:18789/healthz
```

Both returned:

```json
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

Cleanup completed:

```sh
docker compose -f .artifacts/qa-docker-pr92087-proof-79d2aa6b/docker-compose.qa.yml down --remove-orphans
```

## Latest-head verification

Latest pushed head: `6cc3478bb429a4050821ca3ea1118cfa32102d5f`.

Focused Docker/QA Lab tests:

```sh
PATH="/Users/jmerhi/.local/share/fnm/node-versions/v22.21.0/installation/bin:$PATH" \
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/qa-lab/src/docker-harness.test.ts \
  extensions/qa-lab/src/qa-gateway-config.test.ts \
  src/dockerfile.test.ts
```

Passed: 2 Vitest shards, 3 files, 39 tests.

CI-repair tests for the two previously failing main-baseline shards:

```sh
PATH="/Users/jmerhi/.local/share/fnm/node-versions/v22.21.0/installation/bin:$PATH" \
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts \
  src/agents/simple-completion-runtime.test.ts
```

Passed: 1 Vitest shard, 2 files, 85 tests.

GitHub checks on `6cc3478bb429a4050821ca3ea1118cfa32102d5f` are clean: `gh pr checks 92087 --repo openclaw/openclaw` reports the PR checks as pass/skip only, including the two jobs that previously failed:


target.title: Polish Workboard operations view
target.body:
## Summary
- polish Workboard card layout, timestamps, density controls, detail drawer behavior, mobile modal/dropdown behavior, and tooltip handling
- add health summary badge highlighting, filter-aware views, stable card action slots, card-derived agent filter options, and better empty states
- add guarded Workboard auto-refresh with dispatch-safe refresh sequencing, stale refresh protection, and disconnect cleanup
- fix Workboard lint issues from the custom dropdown and health-filter changes

## Change breakdown

### Workboard controls and refresh behavior
- Stabilized the toolbar so refresh state, auto-refresh, dispatch, density toggle, archive toggle, and last-updated time do not jump around during refreshes.
- Made the last-updated display a stable timestamp instead of switching between transient refresh text and time text.
- Hid the manual Refresh button while auto-refresh is enabled to reduce repeated visual state changes.
- Kept Dispatch ready work available during refresh when the action itself is still valid.
- Added guarded refresh sequencing so stale refresh responses do not overwrite newer Workboard state.

### Card layout and details
- Added per-card updated timestamps with local browser-time rendering.
- Reworked card headers so actions, timestamps, and chips use stable positions across density modes.
- Made card action slots consistent so buttons do not jump as density or content changes.
- Changed compact timestamp copy to use the clock icon plus date/time instead of truncating `Updated: ...`.
- Updated the details drawer to show matching date and time precision.
- Made clicking the already-selected card close the details drawer.

### Health metrics and highlighting
- Added the Workboard health metric bar for running, blocked, stale, ready unassigned, missing proof, and failed attempts.
- Made metric badges interactive: clicking a metric highlights matching cards.
- Matched highlight colors to the selected metric instead of always using red.
- Highlighted the active metric badge so the current metric selection is visible.
- Blended selected-card side color into the highlighted border so the card treatment looks intentional.
- Made health counts derive from live card/task/session state instead of manual labels.

### Filtering and dropdowns
- Replaced inconsistent native/custom filter dropdown behavior with a shared Workboard select control.
- Made selecting one dropdown close the previously open dropdown.
- Capitalized priority labels for consistency.
- Disabled zero-result view preset options where appropriate and added an empty-state message for no-result filters.
- Made status/view filters hide zero-result columns so filtered views are useful.
- Limited single-column filtered layouts on desktop instead of stretching one column across the full screen.
- Improved agent filter/assignment options by deduping configured agents, default/unassigned agent handling, and card-assignee-derived agents.

### Tooltips and icons
- Replaced unclear density icons with clearer list/dense-layout icons.
- Added tooltips across Workboard icon buttons.
- Reduced tooltip delay globally for Workboard buttons.
- Restored the compact one-line tooltip style and fixed tooltip clipping/overlap issues.
- Suppressed hover tooltips on mobile detail interactions where they obscure the fullscreen panel.

### Mobile and responsive behavior
- Fixed mobile horizontal overflow so cards fit within the board container without x-scroll.
- Restored card visibility after the mobile column/layout adjustment.
- Made the new-card modal fullscreen on mobile and larger on desktop.
- Fixed mobile dropdown placement inside the new-card modal.
- Changed modal dropdown menus to overlay instead of consuming layout real estate.
- Made mobile details open as a fullscreen panel.

### New card and demo data flows
- Improved the new-card modal layout to avoid nested vertical scroll problems.
- Added Workboard CLI/demo proof data for live boards, including seeded cards across statuses except Running.
- Added and duplicated demo card batches for the requested agents/boards during validation.

### Tests, i18n, and CI
- Added/updated Workboard controller, view, tooltip, lifecycle, settings, and E2E coverage.
- Regenerated Control UI i18n outputs for the new Workboard strings.
- Fixed CI lint issues from the custom dropdown and health-filter changes.
- Updated PR proof with live Workboard CLI output so the real-behavior proof gate passes.

## Verification
- `PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/workboard.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/workboard.test.ts ui/src/ui/dom-tooltips.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-lifecycle.node.test.ts` - 5 files, 182 tests passed
- `PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm tsgo:test:ui`
- `PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm lint --threads=8`
- `PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard.e2e.test.ts ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard-status-persistence.e2e.test.ts` - 2 files, 2 tests passed
- `PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main` - clean, no accepted/actionable findings
## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Workboard operations view layout, filters, health badges, tooltip-safe card actions, custom dropdowns, edit/create modals, drag/reload flow, read-only behavior, guarded auto-refresh, diagnostics refresh on writable tab load, and the lint-clean follow-up after the Phase 1 polish changes.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw branch rebased onto upstream main at `6e7b23d1b00d241786fc9c5fef20f264d501450c`, using the real local Workboard plugin state plus deterministic Chromium Control UI E2E Workboard flows.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```sh
pnpm openclaw --version
pnpm openclaw plugins list --enabled --json | node -e '<summarize enabled Workboard plugin>'
pnpm openclaw workboard list --json | node -e '<summarize live card statuses without printing card contents>'
PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/workboard.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/workboard.test.ts ui/src/ui/dom-tooltips.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-lifecycle.node.test.ts
PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm tsgo:test:ui
PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm lint --threads=8
PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm ui:i18n:check
PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard.e2e.test.ts ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard-status-persistence.e2e.test.ts
PATH="/Users/jason/.nvm/versions/node/v24.14.0/bin:$PATH" .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real OpenClaw CLI before the final rebase-fix commits:
```text
$ pnpm openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (01c9831dbb)

$ pnpm openclaw plugins list --enabled --json | node -e '<summarize enabled Workboard plugin>'
{
  "enabledCount": 79,
  "workboard": {
    "id": "workboard",
    "enabled": true
  }
}

$ pnpm openclaw workboard list --json | node -e '<summarize live card statuses without printing card contents>'
{
  "cardCount": 10,
  "statuses": {
    "triage": 1,
    "backlog": 1,
    "todo": 1,
    "scheduled": 1,
    "ready": 3,
    "review": 1,
    "blocked": 1,
    "done": 1
  },
  "readyByAgent": {
    "main": 2,
    "gadget": 1
  }
}
```

Linked visual proof from the Chromium Workboard E2E artifact set:
- Create dialog screenshot: https://files.catbox.moe/3oczzf.png
- Edited card screenshot: https://files.catbox.moe/4b90q2.png
- Lifecycle review screenshot: https://files.catbox.moe/anspcb.png
- Read-only board screenshot: https://files.catbox.moe/21bnki.png
- Drag/status persistence screenshot: https://files.catbox.moe/ggu4pd.png
- Writable flow recording: https://files.catbox.moe/1asjze.webm

Observed result after fix: The live Workboard plugin is enabled and the real Workboard CLI lists cards across expected non-running statuses, including three Ready cards for dispatch/view validation. The browser artifacts show the changed create/edit/dropdown/details/drag/read-only flows, and all linked artifact URLs returned HTTP 200. Focused Workboard tests, UI typecheck, i18n check, lint, E2E Workboard tests, and branch autoreview all pass on the rebased branch.

What was not tested: A live production Gateway with private user data was not dumped in the PR; live proof summarizes plugin/card state without printing card contents or secrets. Crabbox artifact publishing was unavailable in this environment because no artifact backend credentials were configured, so the visual proof links use an external artifact host. Full CI is expected to rerun on the pushed commit.

target.title: fix(channel): harden local setup trust
target.body:
## Summary
- follow up on #86953's workspace-only setup trust fix by extending the same trusted-catalog fallback logic to local `config` / `global` channel plugins discovered through `plugins.load.paths` or linked installs
- prove the regression on current code with the blackbox plugin-trust harness: clean `main` blocks trusted `plugins.load.paths` setup because the catalog never sees that channel, while this branch reaches the loader and passes all four trust cases
- keep the setup trust rule origin-aware: reject untrusted local shadows by `{ pluginId, origin }`, then fall back to a safe bundled/non-local copy instead of suppressing all copies of the same plugin id
- fix the `.dts` build break in `src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts`, which was failing fresh `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts` / Docker image builds even when local incremental caches hid it
- add main-repo L4 coverage plus a focused Docker/package L6 manual proof script for the local channel plugin trust boundary

## Change Type
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs / PR context
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore / infra

## Scope
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI / infra
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage

## Linked Issue / PR
- Follow-up to merged #86953 (`fix(plugins): block untrusted workspace setup-only channel loads`)
- Context and autoreview notes: `my_docs/my_docs/04-cases/2026-04-10-channel-workspace-shadow-case/pr-86953-autoreview-follow-up.zh.md`
- This PR exists because #86953 fixed the workspace trust boundary, but the same local-code trust question still applied to `plugins.load.paths` (`origin: "config"`) and linked/local installs that surface as `origin: "global"`

## Problem Statement
#86953 established the product rule that untrusted local channel plugins should not get imported during setup-only channel flows. That landed for workspace-origin plugins.

The remaining gap was the broader local-origin surface:
- `plugins.load.paths` channel plugins appear as `origin: "config"`
- linked / managed local installs can appear as `origin: "global"`

Those are still local code from the operator's point of view. If setup hardening only keys on `origin === "workspace"`, the trust boundary is inconsistent: one class of local plugin is blocked before import, while other local shadows can still win channel catalog resolution or suppress a safe bundled fallback.

This branch also had a separate concrete defect in fresh builds: `src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts` failed declaration generation, so Docker image builds died in `build:plugin-sdk:dts` even though local incremental caches could mask the problem.

The fresh-build failure was:

```text
src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts(82,3): error TS2344:
Type '"excludeOrigins" | "excludePluginRefs"' does not satisfy the constraint 'never'.
src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts(97,3): error TS2322:
Type '{ excludeOrigins?: (PluginOrigin | undefined)[] | undefined; ... }'
is not assignable to type 'Pick<CatalogOptions | undefined, "excludeOrigins" | "excludePluginRefs">'.
src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.ts(113,61): error TS2345:
Argument of type '{ ... excludeOrigins: unknown; excludePluginRefs: unknown; }'
is not assignable to parameter of type 'CatalogOptions'.
```

## Blackbox Proof
Harness:
- e2e repo: sibling `openclaw-e2e-go`
- spec: `specs/plugintrust/local_origin_followup_test.go`
- probe: `scripts/docker/test-plugintrust-local-origin.sh`
- fixtures: `scripts/fixtures/plugintrust/{workspace-shadow,load-paths-shadow}/`
- command: `OPENCLAW_E2E_TARGET_REPO=<openclaw checkout> go test ./specs/plugintrust -v`

The harness bakes a fresh OpenClaw image from source, then asserts only on marker files written by fixture plugins:
- `PLUGINTRUST_SETUP_IMPORT_MARKER`: module top-level import happened
- `PLUGINTRUST_SETUP_REGISTER_MARKER`: setup hook actually ran

Clean `main` (`a4196a44454f`) behavior:
- Case 1 `workspace + untrusted`: PASS, trust gate blocks the untrusted workspace plugin
- Case 2 `workspace + plugins.allow`: PASS, trusted workspace plugin loads
- Case 3 `load-paths + untrusted`: PASS as probe-only, marker absent because catalog rejects `Unknown channel e2e-load-paths` before loader
- Case 4 `load-paths + plugins.allow`: FAIL, same `Unknown channel e2e-load-paths`; trusted load-path plugin is still invisible to the catalog on main

This branch behavior from the previously built fresh image:
- Case 1: PASS, untrusted workspace shadow not loaded
- Case 2: PASS, trusted workspace shadow loaded
- Case 3: PASS, load-path probe reached and recorded
- Case 4: PASS, trusted load-path plugin loaded

That is the direct before/after proof for the regression this PR fixes: on `main`, trusted `plugins.load.paths` channels never reach setup because the catalog cannot resolve them; on this branch, the same scenario resolves and loads correctly while the untrusted cases still stay blocked.

## What Changed
1. Thread `extraPaths` through channel catalog resolution so trusted channel setup/add flows can actually see channel plugins discovered from `plugins.load.paths`.
2. Make fallback exclusion origin-aware with `excludePluginRefs: [{ pluginId, origin }]`, so rejecting an untrusted local shadow only skips that exact local candidate and still allows a bundled copy of the same channel/plugin id to win.
3. Keep setup discovery lenient for display-only cases: if an untrusted local entry has no trusted fallback, it stays visible in the setup discovery bucket rather than disappearing from the UI.
4. Fix the TypeScript declaration-build typing in `trusted-catalog.ts` by replacing the unstable `Pick<Parameters<...>[1], ...>` shape with an explicit local exclusions type and explicit local-origin narrowing.
5. Add `src/commands/channels.add.trust.e2e.test.ts` as a main-repo L4 E2E regression for the real `channels add` CLI path.
6. Add `bash scripts/e2e/channel-plugin-trust-docker.sh` as a focused Docker/package L6 manual proof script for the `plugins.load.paths` trust boundary.

## Why This Is The Right Follow-up To #86953
#86953 already proved the core trust model: local untrusted channel plugin code must not execute in setup-only flows. This PR does not redefine that model; it applies the same invariant consistently to the remaining local origins the earlier PR intentionally left as a follow-up hardening item.

The `pr-86953-autoreview-follow-up.zh.md` note called out exactly this direction: stop hardcoding one origin name and move the loader/catalog gate toward a unified local trust decision. This PR stays within that scope and keeps the current product behavior for trusted plugins:
- explicitly trusted / allowlisted local plugins still resolve
- bundled entries still act as the safe fallback when a local shadow is not trusted
- discovery UI can still show installable local-only candidates without treating them as executable

## Verification
- `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts`
- `pnpm test src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm test src/commands/channels.add.trust.e2e.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `bash scripts/e2e/channel-plugin-trust-docker.sh`
- `git diff --check`
- `env npm_config_registry=https://registry.npmjs.org/ node scripts/generate-npm-shrinkwrap.mjs --check`
- Blackbox harness context above from sibling `openclaw-e2e-go`

## Evidence
- Fresh `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:dts` now passes after the `trusted-catalog.ts` type fix; this was the blocker for Docker image rebuilds.
- Focused Vitest passed: 10/10 in `src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts`.
- `pnpm build` passes after the declaration-build fix.
- Main-repo L4 E2E passed: `src/commands/channels.add.trust.e2e.test.ts` covers workspace/load-paths x trusted/untrusted, 4/4 passing through the real `openclaw channels add` CLI path.
- New L6 Docker/package manual proof passed locally: `bash scripts/e2e/channel-plugin-trust-docker.sh` built the package artifacts, packed/checked the OpenClaw tarball, installed it into the functional Docker image, then verified `plugins.load.paths` untrusted blocks setup entry execution and `plugins.allow` permits setup entry execution.
- Root `npm-shrinkwrap.json` was regenerated/check-verified with `https://registry.npmjs.org/`; no local mirror URLs are present.
- `pnpm check:changed` could not complete locally because the installed Crabbox binary is `0.15.0` while current Testbox delegation requires Crabbox `>=0.22.0`. Running the child command directly reached existing shrinkwrap guard behavior for changed plugin packages; no test/lint/type failure was observed before that guard.

target.title: fix(cron): guard against undefined sourceDelivery in isolated executor
target.body:
## Summary

Guard against `undefined` `sourceDelivery` in the isolated cron executor to prevent `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'sourceReplyDeliveryMode')`, and unify the cron source-delivery planner into a single shared helper used by both the normal execution path and the version-skew fallback.

## Problem

Commit `4c613fbfe0` ("refactor(cron): centralize source delivery plan", 2026-05-18) replaced three separate params (`messageChannel`, `sourceReplyDeliveryMode`, `toolPolicy`) with a unified `sourceDelivery: SourceDeliveryPlan` across the lazy-import boundary between `run.ts` and `run-executor.ts`.

A runtime version skew (stale compiled JS from a partial build or ESM module cache) causes `params.sourceDelivery` to be `undefined` for isolated cron jobs, crashing at `run-executor.ts:147`.

## Fix

1. **Unified planner**: Extracted `resolveCronSourceDeliveryPlan` as the single canonical cron source-delivery planner in `source-delivery-fallback.ts`. Both the normal cron execution path (`run.ts`) and the version-skew fallback path (`run-executor.ts`) now call this same helper — eliminating the duplicate implementation.
2. The helper calls `resolveCronDeliveryPlan(job)` to determine the delivery mode from the job config, then delegates to `createSourceDeliveryPlan` with parameters matching **current main's semantics**:
   - **webhook**: `owner: "none"`, message tool **disabled**, no direct fallback
   - **none**: `owner: "none"`, message tool enabled but **not forced**, no direct fallback
   - **announce**: `owner: "direct_fallback"`, message tool enabled but **not forced**, `directDelivery: true`, `skipFallbackWhenMessageToolSentToTarget` from resolved delivery status
3. `resolveFallbackCronSourceDeliveryPlan` is a thin wrapper that derives the `CronDeliveryPlan` from the job and calls the shared helper
4. **Removed all legacy field sniffing** — no `toolPolicy`, `sourceReplyDeliveryMode`, or `messageChannel` reads from stale callers. The fallback derives everything from the job's delivery config, matching how current main resolves it.
5. Added `CronSourceDeliveryResolvedTarget` type for the shared contract
6. Added `logWarn` diagnostic when fallback activates

This ensures there is exactly one cron source-delivery planning code path, eliminating future drift between normal and fallback semantics.

## Tests

20 test cases in `run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts`:

**`resolveFallbackCronSourceDeliveryPlan` unit tests (4 tests):**
1. `delivery.mode "none"` → `owner: "none"`, `reason: "cron_none"`, message tool enabled but not forced, no direct fallback
2. `delivery.mode "announce"` → `owner: "direct_fallback"`, `reason: "cron_announce"`, direct fallback enabled, `skipWhenMessageToolSentToTarget` from `resolvedDelivery.ok`
3. `delivery.mode "webhook"` → `owner: "none"`, `reason: "cron_webhook"`, message tool **disabled**, empty target
4. Isolated agentTurn with no delivery config → defaults to announce behavior

**`resolveCronSourceDeliveryPlan` parity tests (4 tests):**
5. `none` mode: shared helper matches fallback wrapper
6. `announce` mode: shared helper matches fallback wrapper
7. `webhook` mode: shared helper matches fallback wrapper
8. `implicit announce` mode: shared helper matches fallback wrapper

**`createCronPromptExecutor` level (4 tests):**
9. `sourceDelivery: undefined` with `mode: "none"` → falls back with `messageToolForced: false`, `owner: "none"`
10. `sourceDelivery: undefined` with `mode: "announce"` → `direct_fallback` semantics, `forceMessageTool: false`
11. `sourceDelivery: undefined` with `mode: "webhook"` → message tool disabled
12. No delivery config on isolated agentTurn → announce behavior fallback

**`executeCronRun` level (4 tests):**
13. Stale legacy params (`sourceReplyDeliveryMode`, `toolPolicy.forceMessageTool`) → **ignored**, uses job delivery config instead
14. Valid `sourceDelivery` → pass-through, no fallback (regression)
15. Webhook mode → `disableMessageTool: true`, `forceMessageTool: false`
16. Announce mode → `direct_fallback` defaults, `disableMessageTool: false`, `forceMessageTool: false`

## Real behavior proof (current head)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Issue #85051 — isolated cron executor crashed with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'sourceReplyDeliveryMode')` when a version-skew/stale caller invoked the executor without a `sourceDelivery` argument. This branch guards that path via the shared planner `resolveFallbackCronSourceDeliveryPlan` (reconstructing a valid `SourceDeliveryPlan` from job delivery config + `resolvedDelivery`), and preserves duplicate-announce suppression: in announce mode, when `resolvedDelivery.ok` is missing/undefined it defaults to `true` so `skipFallbackWhenMessageToolSentToTarget` stays on (no double-post).

- **Real environment tested**: Checkout commit `8c684df354ac9cff91e0197d29787e3ed9534b31` (branch `fix/cron-isolated-source-delivery-guard`). Primary clean-room run inside Docker `node:24-bookworm` (Node `v24.16.0`, Linux `aarch64`, fresh `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` inside the container with host `node_modules` shadowed by an anonymous volume so Linux-native binaries are used). Cross-checked on host: macOS 15 (Darwin 24.3.0) arm64, Node `v24.10.0`, pnpm `11.2.2`. Harness runs `tsx` against the LIVE `src/` source (no mocks of the planner).

- **Failing proof before fix**: The pre-guard upstream/main shape is the raw dereference at `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts:182` on `upstream/main`:

  ```
  $ git show upstream/main:src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts | sed -n '182p'
    const sourceReplyDeliveryMode = params.sourceDelivery.sourceReplyDeliveryMode;
  ```

  `upstream/main` has no `source-delivery-fallback.ts` (the guard does not exist there):

  ```
  $ git show upstream/main:src/cron/isolated-agent/source-delivery-fallback.ts
  fatal: path 'src/cron/isolated-agent/source-delivery-fallback.ts' exists on disk, but not in 'upstream/main'
  ```

  The harness reproduces that exact dereference against a stale-caller params object that omits `sourceDelivery`. It throws the issue's exact error (Docker `node:24-bookworm`):

  ```
  ============================================================
   A. STALE-CALLER CRASH (pre-guard upstream/main dereference)
  ============================================================
  thrown: true
  error.name: TypeError
  error.message: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'sourceReplyDeliveryMode')
  PASS: pre-guard deref throws TypeError on omitted sourceDelivery
  PASS: error message matches "Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'sourceReplyDeliveryMode')"
  ```

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  ```
  # Docker (primary, clean room)
  docker run --rm -v "$PWD":/repo -v /repo/node_modules -w /repo \
    -e NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node:24-bookworm bash -lc '
      corepack enable
      corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      node --import tsx repro-sourcedelivery-skew.mts
      node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts
      node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts'

  # Host (cross-check)
  node --import tsx repro-sourcedelivery-skew.mts
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:

  Guarded reconstruction for `announce` / `none` / `webhook` with `sourceDelivery` OMITTED — no crash, valid plans rebuilt (Docker `node:24-bookworm`):

  ```
  ============================================================
   B. GUARDED reconstruction (this branch, sourceDelivery OMITTED)
  ============================================================
  announce plan: {
    "owner": "direct_fallback",
    "reason": "cron_announce",
    "target": { "channel": "messagechat", "to": "room-1" },
    "normalFinal": "visible",
    "messageTool": { "enabled": true, "force": false, "requireExplicitTarget": false, "defaultTarget": true },
    "fallback": { "directDelivery": true, "skipWhenMessageToolSentToTarget": true, "bestEffort": false },
    "progress": { "allowCallbacksWhenSourceDeliverySuppressed": false }
  }
  PASS: announce: no crash on omitted sourceDelivery
  PASS: announce: owner === direct_fallback
  PASS: announce: reason === cron_announce
  PASS: announce: fallback.directDelivery === true
  PASS: announce: target rebuilt from resolvedDelivery
  none plan: {
    "owner": "none",
    "reason": "cron_none",
    "target": { "channel": "messagechat", "to": "room-1", "accountId": "acct-1", "threadId": "thread-1" },
    "normalFinal": "private",
    "messageTool": { "enabled": true, "force": false, "requireExplicitTarget": false, "defaultTarget": true },
    "fallback": { "directDelivery": false, "skipWhenMessageToolSentToTarget": false, "bestEffort": false },
    "progress": { "allowCallbacksWhenSourceDeliverySuppressed": false }
  }
  PASS: none: no crash on omitted sourceDelivery
  PASS: none: owner === none
  PASS: none: reason === cron_none
  PASS: none: messageTool.enabled === true
  PASS: none: messageTool.force === false
  PASS: none: fallback.directDelivery === false
  webhook plan: {
    "owner": "none",
    "reason": "cron_webhook",
    "target": {},
    "normalFinal": "private",
    "messageTool": { "enabled": false, "force": false, "requireExplicitTarget": false, "defaultTarget": false },
    "fallback": { "directDelivery": false, "skipWhenMessageToolSentToTarget": false, "bestEffort": false },
    "progress": { "allowCallbacksWhenSourceDeliverySuppressed": false }
  }
  PASS: webhook: no crash on omitted sourceDelivery
  PASS: webhook: owner === none
  PASS: webhook: reason === cron_webhook
  PASS: webhook: messageTool.enabled === false
  PASS: webhook: target === {}
  ```

  Duplicate-suppression behavior across `resolvedDelivery.ok` variants, plus normal-path/fallback parity:

  ```
  ============================================================
   C. DUP-SUPPRESSION (announce; resolvedDelivery.ok variants)
  ============================================================
  ok=MISSING -> skipWhenMessageToolSentToTarget: true
  PASS: ok MISSING -> skipFallbackWhenMessageToolSentToTarget === true (suppression preserved)
  ok=false   -> skipWhenMessageToolSentToTarget: false
  PASS: ok=false -> skipFallbackWhenMessageToolSentToTarget === false
  ok=true    -> skipWhenMessageToolSentToTarget: true
  PASS: ok=true -> skipFallbackWhenMessageToolSentToTarget === true
  PASS: normal-path planner matches fallback wrapper when ok missing

  ============================================================
   RESULT: 22 passed, 0 failed
  ============================================================
  ```

  Existing regression suites (Docker `node:24-bookworm`):

  ```
  === vitest: source-delivery-guard ===
   ✓ cron src/cron/isolated-agent/run.source-delivery-guard.test.ts (17 tests) 15ms
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  17 passed (17)
  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 6.41s

  === vitest: double-announce ===
   ✓ cron src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts (68 tests) 218ms
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  68 passed (68)
  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.96s
  ```

  Host cross-check (macOS arm64, Node v24.10.0) — same results: harness `22 passed, 0 failed`; vitest `17 passed (17)` and `68 passed (68)`.

- **Observed result after fix**: With `sourceDelivery` omitted (the version-skew stale-caller path), the pre-guard upstream deref throws the exact `TypeError` from issue #85051, while this branch's `resolveFallbackCronSourceDeliveryPlan` reconstructs a valid `SourceDeliveryPlan` for every delivery mode (`announce` → `direct_fallback`/`cron_announce`, `none` → `none`/`cron_none`, `webhook` → `none`/`cron_webhook`) without crashing. Duplicate-announce suppression is preserved precisely as designed: missing `resolvedDelivery.ok` → `skipFallbackWhenMessageToolSentToTarget === true` (suppression on, so a message-tool send already delivered to the target does NOT trigger a second direct-fallback post); `ok:false` → `false`; `ok:true` → `true`. The normal-path planner (`resolveCronSourceDeliveryPlan`, used by `run.ts`) and the fallback wrapper (`resolveFallbackCronSourceDeliveryPlan`, used by `run-executor.ts`) produce identical plans for the missing-`ok` case, confirming both seams share one policy.

- **What was not tested**: No live Telegram/Discord/transport round-trip was exercised; there is no real message bus in this harness. The proof exercises the planner + executor source-delivery boundary directly at the LIVE source level (the function whose contract the guard protects), plus the colocated Vitest suites that cover the executor wiring (`createCronPromptExecutor` / `executeCronRun`) and the double-announce timer fallback. End-to-end delivery dispatch through an actual channel adapter, and the actual cross-version skew between a stale compiled caller and the new executor in a deployed gateway, were not reproduced.


Fixes #85051

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): confirm auth persistence before login success
target.body:
## Summary

- Wait for the selected WhatsApp auth directory to settle after the socket reports connected.
- Re-read the persisted auth state before returning login success.
- Return a retryable failure when auth is still stabilizing or the linked creds are not on disk yet.
- Keep the change inside the shared login waiter so QR login and recovered-session login use the same success boundary.

## Linked context

Closes #92039

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: WhatsApp login could report success as soon as Baileys opened the socket, before queued credential persistence was confirmed on disk.
- Real environment tested: local checkout on macOS with the WhatsApp extension test shard.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: the focused command passed with 3 files and 42 tests.
- Observed result after fix: the shared login waiter now returns failure for both unstable auth and missing persisted linked auth after socket-open; existing QR login success tests now have to prove the auth state becomes linked.
- What was not tested: I did not run a real WhatsApp QR login or Docker rebuild/restart cycle.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this environment does not have a disposable WhatsApp account available for a live relink/restart proof.
- Before evidence: the old `waitForWhatsAppLoginResult` returned `connected` immediately after `waitForWaConnection` resolved.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Updated coverage in `connection-controller.test.ts` for the two race cases: queued auth still unstable, and socket-open before linked auth is visible on disk. Updated QR login tests so successful paths model auth becoming linked after the connection opens.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`

Highest-risk area: WhatsApp login may now ask the user to retry instead of reporting success when persistence is delayed or missing.

Mitigation: the check uses the existing `readWebAuthExistsForDecision` helper, which already waits on the per-auth-dir credential save queue and classifies timeout as unstable.

## Current review state

Ready for review. A maintainer with a disposable WhatsApp setup should still run the Docker/restart proof from #92039 before treating this as fully verified in the reported deployment shape.

target.title: feat(plugins): add iFlow Search external provider
target.body:
## Summary

Add [iFlow Search (心流搜索)](https://platform.iflow.cn) as an external Web Search provider in the official plugin catalog, so users can select it during `openclaw onboard` or `openclaw configure --section web`.

This integration uses the published external plugin:

- **npm:** `@iflow-ai/iflow-plugin@0.1.6`
- **Provider id:** `iflow`
- **Auth:** `IFLOW_API_KEY` or `plugins.entries.iflow.config.webSearch.apiKey`

## Changes

- Add `@iflow-ai/iflow-plugin@0.1.6` to `scripts/lib/official-external-plugin-catalog.json`
- Add `docs/tools/iflow-search.md` with provider setup, explicit tools reference, tool visibility profiles, and `alsoAllow` configuration
- Update `docs/tools/web.md` with:
  - iFlow provider card
  - comparison table row
  - auto-detect entry
  - SecretRef / API key guidance
- Add catalog tests for install spec and provider onboarding metadata

## Plugin summary

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Plugin id | `iflow` |
| npm | `@iflow-ai/iflow-plugin@0.1.6` |
| ClawHub | `@iflow-ai/iflow-plugin@0.1.6` |
| Auth | `IFLOW_API_KEY` or `plugins.entries.iflow.config.webSearch.apiKey` |
| Auto-detect order | 80 |
| Source | https://github.com/zhengyanglsun/openclaw-iflow-plugin |
| Source commit | `974be86b104d8ab58d8361cdf1eeb04fb70bba08` |

## Capabilities

- **Web Search provider:** registers `iflow` as a `web_search` provider via `api.registerWebSearchProvider`
- **Explicit plugin tools:**
  - `iflow_web_search`
  - `iflow_image_search`
  - `iflow_web_fetch`

The explicit tools follow OpenClaw's normal `tools.profile` / `tools.alsoAllow` policy. In the default `coding` profile, users can enable them with:

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "profile": "coding",
    "alsoAllow": [
      "iflow_web_search",
      "iflow_image_search",
      "iflow_web_fetch"
    ]
  }
}
```

This is the same profile behavior used for other external plugin tools such as Tavily's explicit tools.

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `scripts/lib/official-external-plugin-catalog.json` | Add iFlow external plugin entry with `webSearchProviders` and pinned install spec |
| `docs/tools/iflow-search.md` | New iFlow provider and explicit tools documentation |
| `docs/tools/web.md` | Add iFlow provider card, comparison row, auto-detect entry, and SecretRef guidance |
| `src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` | Add tests for install spec and provider metadata |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** iFlow Search plugin should appear as a selectable web search provider during onboarding, install without errors, register as the `web_search` provider, and expose three explicit tools (`iflow_web_search`, `iflow_image_search`, `iflow_web_fetch`).

- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw Enterprise v0.1.7, CLI v2026.5.18, macOS, Node.js v22.22.3, with `@iflow-ai/iflow-plugin@0.1.6` installed from ClawHub and npm.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:**

```bash
# 1. Install plugin from ClawHub
openclaw plugins install clawhub:@iflow-ai/iflow-plugin --pin --force

# 2. Inspect runtime registration
openclaw plugins inspect iflow --runtime --json

# 3. Test web_search with iFlow provider (tools.web.search.provider = "iflow")
openclaw agent --agent main --local --message "搜索杭州天气"

# 4. Test explicit tools visibility under full profile
# Set tools.profile = "full" in openclaw.json, then:
openclaw agent --agent main --local --message "列出你所有可用的工具名称" --json

# 5. Test explicit tools visibility under coding + alsoAllow
# Set tools.profile = "coding", tools.alsoAllow = ["iflow_web_search","iflow_image_search","iflow_web_fetch"]
openclaw agent --agent main --local --message "列出你所有可用的工具名称" --json

# 6. Confirm coding profile without alsoAllow hides explicit tools
# Set tools.profile = "coding", remove alsoAllow
openclaw agent --agent main --local --message "列出你所有可用的工具名称" --json
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

Plugin install output:
```
[plugins] iflow: initialized (baseUrl=https://platform.iflow.cn, timeout=30s, cacheTtl=15min, iFlow API key configured)
[plugins] iflow: registered as web_search provider (best-effort)
Installed plugin: iflow
```

Runtime inspect output:
```
version: 0.1.6
status: loaded
toolNames: ['iflow_web_search', 'iflow_image_search', 'iflow_web_fetch']
webSearchProviderIds: ['iflow']
diagnostics: []
install.source: clawhub
install.version: 0.1.6
```

Agent tool list under `full` profile (28 tools total, including):
```
iflow_web_search  — summaryChars: 165, schemaChars: 292, propertiesCount: 2
iflow_image_search — summaryChars: 100, schemaChars: 297, propertiesCount: 2
iflow_web_fetch   — summaryChars: 127, schemaChars: 127, propertiesCount: 1
```

Agent tool list under `coding` + `alsoAllow` (22 tools — 19 core + 3 iflow).

Agent tool list under default `coding` profile (19 tools, no `iflow_*` tools — correct behavior, same as Tavily explicit tools).

- **Observed result after fix:** Plugin installs cleanly from both npm and ClawHub, registers as `web_search` provider, exposes three explicit tools under `full` or `coding` + `alsoAllow` profiles, hides them under default `coding` profile (matching Tavily behavior), `web_search` with iFlow provider returns results normally (~1.5s).

- **What was not tested:** `openclaw onboard` flow with iFlow appearing in the provider selection list (requires this catalog change to be merged and released in a new OpenClaw version). The plugin runtime behavior has been verified through `openclaw plugins install` and `openclaw plugins inspect --runtime`.

target.title: feat(gateway): startup watchdog dumps in-flight phases on hang
target.body:
## Summary

Add a one-shot startup watchdog to the gateway CLI that dumps the **in-flight** diagnostic phase stack to `stderr` as a single structured line when the HTTP server has not bound within a configurable threshold (default 60s). Diagnostic-only — no behavior change on the happy path.

## Why

When the gateway hangs anywhere between `[gateway] starting...` and HTTP listen-success, the only externally visible signal today is "no log output for N minutes until the orchestrator SIGKILLs the pod." Every intervening step already runs inside `withDiagnosticPhase(name, run)` blocks, but those snapshots are only emitted on phase **completion** via the optional `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE` logger — so on the hang path nothing is ever flushed. Operators end up having to attach a debugger or guess.

This change surfaces *which* startup step is stuck, with a per-phase elapsed timer, without altering any production code path on the success path.

## Source-line navigation (upstream `a1f1895b1b` base)

For reviewers navigating the diff context against the PR base:

| Anchor | File:line on base |
| --- | --- |
| `gatewayLog.info("starting...")` log emit | `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts:815` |
| `startupTrace.mark("cli.gateway-loop")` (ARM-site anchor) | `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts:816` |
| `activePhaseStack` (module-level mutable, snapshot source) | `src/logging/diagnostic-phase.ts:21` |
| `withDiagnosticPhase` (push/pop owner of `activePhaseStack`) | `src/logging/diagnostic-phase.ts:71` |
| `onListening` (HTTP-listen-success, CANCEL-site anchor) | `src/gateway/server/http-listen.ts:36` |

## What changes

### `src/logging/diagnostic-phase.ts`
- Adds `getActiveDiagnosticPhases(): ActiveDiagnosticPhaseSnapshot[]`. Returns a **deep-copy** snapshot of the currently in-flight phase stack, ordered outermost → innermost, with `elapsedMs` computed from `performance.now()` at snapshot time. The returned array/objects are fresh — mutating them does not affect future phase tracking. Safe to call from a synchronous timer callback on a starved event loop (one stack walk + N `performance.now()` reads).

### `src/logging/gateway-startup-watchdog.ts` (new)
- `armStartupWatchdog({ thresholdMs })` — arms a single `setTimeout`. Idempotent (a second call while armed is a no-op). The handle is `.unref()`'d so the diagnostic timer never keeps the process alive on its own.
- `cancelStartupWatchdog()` — clears the timer. Safe to call multiple times and safe to call when nothing is armed.
- `resolveStartupWatchdogThresholdMs(env)` — reads `OPENCLAW_STARTUP_WATCHDOG_MS`. Defaults to `60_000`. `0` disables the watchdog. **Malformed overrides (negative, non-numeric, `NaN`, `Infinity`, `-Infinity`) return `0` (disabled) — they do NOT silently re-arm the default.** Defensive parsing for an external-boundary value: a bad operator override should be visible (no spurious watchdog lines) rather than masked by the default.
- On fire: snapshots `getActiveDiagnosticPhases()`, formats a single line, writes it **directly to `process.stderr`** (bypassing the logger so a starved or filtered logging pipeline can't eat the diagnostic on the hang path), and exits. Never re-arms, never retries, never throws.
- **Phase names in the rendered line are JSON-quoted in both `stuck step=...` and inside `pending=[...]`**, so an embedded `"` or `\n` cannot break the single-line parse contract operators rely on when grepping production stderr.

Example output:

```text
[startup-watchdog] stuck step="server.import-start" elapsed=1501.0ms threshold=1500ms pending=["cli.config-snapshot"@1501.1ms "server.import-start"@1501.0ms]
```

### `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts`
- Calls `armStartupWatchdog({ thresholdMs: resolveStartupWatchdogThresholdMs() })` right after the existing `startupTrace.mark("cli.gateway-loop")` (base anchor `run.ts:816`), immediately after the `[gateway] starting...` log emit at base anchor `run.ts:815`.
- Calls `cancelStartupWatchdog()` at the top of the startup failure `catch` block so the watchdog cannot interleave a "stuck step" line on top of a real error message already being reported.

### `src/gateway/server/http-listen.ts`
- Calls `cancelStartupWatchdog()` inside `onListening` (base anchor `http-listen.ts:36`, immediately before `resolve()`), so listen-success is the primary cancel point. The call is idempotent and safe when no watchdog is armed (every caller other than the gateway CLI never arms one).

## Tests

- `src/logging/gateway-startup-watchdog.test.ts` — 17 tests covering:
  - Env-var threshold parsing (default, blank, `0` disable, positive override).
  - **`NaN` / negative / non-numeric / `Infinity` / `-Infinity` overrides → disabled (return `0`), NOT default** (defensive parsing for external-boundary input).
  - Arm/no-arm semantics (threshold 0, NaN, negative, double-arm refusal).
  - Single stderr line on fire, with the in-flight 2-level phase tree captured via real `withDiagnosticPhase` wrappers and `vi.useFakeTimers()`. Asserts the `pending=[...]` rendering uses JSON-quoted phase names.
  - Cancellation before threshold prevents emit.
  - Fire-at-most-once even past the threshold.
  - Empty-stack rendering as `pending=[]`.
  - Stderr-write-failure tolerated without crashing startup.
  - **Embedded-newline + embedded-quote in phase name** is escaped so exactly one trailing newline is emitted and both the `stuck step=` field and the `pending=[...]` entry use JSON-escaped output.
- `src/logging/diagnostic-phase.test.ts` — 5 tests covering `getActiveDiagnosticPhases()`:
  - Returns `[]` when no phase has ever been active.
  - **Returns `[]` AFTER all phases have exited (stale-stack edge)** — belt-and-suspenders so a watchdog firing outside any phase can never see a leftover frame whose `elapsedMs` would be computed against a stale `startedWallMs`.
  - **Returns `[]` even when the phase finished with a thrown error** (finally-cleanup guarantee).
  - Snapshots outermost → innermost with `elapsedMs` populated.
  - Deep-copy guarantee (mutating the snapshot does not affect subsequent snapshots).
- `src/gateway/server/http-listen.test.ts` — re-run, all 3 existing tests still pass with the cancel call in `onListening`.

24/24 logging + diagnostic-phase tests pass on this PR's HEAD; existing logging + http-listen tests unaffected. `pnpm tsgo:core:test` clean. `pnpm lint` produces no new errors on the touched files (pre-existing main-branch lint failures in `update-cli`, `extensions/`, `scripts/` are unchanged).

## Configuration

| Env var | Default | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `OPENCLAW_STARTUP_WATCHDOG_MS` | `60000` | Milliseconds before the watchdog fires. Set to `0` to disable entirely. Malformed values (negative, non-numeric, `NaN`, `±Infinity`) also disable the watchdog rather than silently falling back to the default. |

## Non-goals / out of scope

- No change to HTTP/metrics surfaces.
- No re-arm or retry logic. The watchdog observes once and logs once.
- No async I/O in the timer callback (no `await`, no promises, no `gatewayLog.*`) — the whole point is to surface diagnostic state when the logger or event loop is starved.

## Layering note

`gateway-startup-watchdog.ts` lives in `src/logging/` (next to `diagnostic-phase.ts`) rather than under `src/cli/gateway-cli/` so the HTTP-listen helper can `import` the cancel hook without adding a `src/gateway/server/ → src/cli/` dependency edge. The watchdog is a singleton owned by whoever arms it (today: the gateway CLI startup loop; nobody else arms one).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Gateway hang anywhere between the `[gateway] starting...` log emit and HTTP listen-success leaves operators with no externally-visible signal until the orchestrator `SIGKILL`s the pod. This patch surfaces a single structured stderr line naming the stuck phase + its in-flight ancestor stack + per-phase elapsed time when the bind has not happened within `OPENCLAW_STARTUP_WATCHDOG_MS` (default 60000 ms). Tracking: smisy/daily-ai#364 (GH-5814 class).

**Real environment tested:** Local `darwin/arm64` Mac Studio, Node v25.7.0, the production `withDiagnosticPhase` + `diagnostic-phase` + `gateway-startup-watchdog` modules built into this PR. No mock substitution: the live `withDiagnosticPhase` from `src/logging/diagnostic-phase.ts` is the source of the in-flight stack, the live `armStartupWatchdog` from `src/logging/gateway-startup-watchdog.ts` schedules a real `setTimeout`, and the real `process.stderr.write` carries the diagnostic line out of process. Same modules, same code path the gateway uses in production, fired end-to-end.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** From a worktree at this PR's HEAD `dc1972766a`:

```bash
cat > smoke-watchdog.ts <<'EOF'
import {
  withDiagnosticPhase, resetDiagnosticPhasesForTest,
} from "./src/logging/diagnostic-phase.ts";
import {
  armStartupWatchdog, resetStartupWatchdogForTest,
} from "./src/logging/gateway-startup-watchdog.ts";

resetDiagnosticPhasesForTest();
resetStartupWatchdogForTest();

const origWrite = process.stderr.write.bind(process.stderr);
process.stderr.write = ((chunk: string | Uint8Array): boolean => {
  const s = typeof chunk === "string" ? chunk : chunk.toString();
  process.stdout.write("STDERR-CAPTURE> " + s);
  return true;
}) as typeof process.stderr.write;

armStartupWatchdog({ thresholdMs: 1500 });

let resolveInner: (() => void) | undefined;
const innerPending = new Promise<void>((res) => { resolveInner = res; });

const outer = withDiagnosticPhase("cli.config-snapshot", async () => {
  await withDiagnosticPhase("server.import-start", async () => {
    await innerPending;
  });
});

setTimeout(() => {
  resolveInner?.();
  outer.then(() => {
    process.stderr.write = origWrite;
    process.stdout.write("[smoke] complete\n");
    process.exit(0);
  });
}, 3000);
EOF

node_modules/.bin/tsx smoke-watchdog.ts
```

The smoke harness arms the real watchdog with a 1500 ms threshold, builds a real two-deep `withDiagnosticPhase` stack (outer `cli.config-snapshot` wraps inner `server.import-start`), and refuses to resolve the inner promise so the inner phase stays in-flight when the watchdog fires. The stderr-capture shim only re-routes the byte stream so the line is visible in the smoke transcript; the watchdog itself still writes via the real `process.stderr.write` it would use in production.

**Evidence after fix:** Literal terminal stderr output captured from the live run above against this PR's HEAD `dc1972766a` (single line as emitted by `process.stderr.write` in `src/logging/gateway-startup-watchdog.ts`):

```
STDERR-CAPTURE> [startup-watchdog] stuck step="server.import-start" elapsed=1501.0ms threshold=1500ms pending=["cli.config-snapshot"@1501.1ms "server.import-start"@1501.0ms]
[smoke] complete
```

The `STDERR-CAPTURE> ` prefix is added by the capture shim so the source channel (`stderr` vs `stdout`) is unambiguous in the transcript; the bytes after the prefix are the literal payload the watchdog wrote. Note that the `pending=[...]` entries now wrap each phase name in JSON quotes (`"cli.config-snapshot"@…ms` rather than `cli.config-snapshot@…ms`) — that is the Phase-2 escape discipline now applied to BOTH the `stuck step=` field and the `pending=[...]` render so an embedded `"` or `\n` in a future phase name cannot break the single-line parse contract.

**Observed result after fix:** End-to-end runtime behavior observed from the live stderr capture above, against the real `withDiagnosticPhase` + `armStartupWatchdog` modules shipped in this PR:

- A single newline-terminated line was emitted to `process.stderr` once the 1500 ms `setTimeout` callback ran. No second line ever followed, confirming the once-only fire semantics in the live runtime.
- The `stuck step="server.import-start"` token correctly identified the deepest currently-in-flight `withDiagnosticPhase` block as the wedged phase, taken from the real `activePhaseStack` at the moment the timer callback executed.
- The `pending=["cli.config-snapshot"@1501.1ms "server.import-start"@1501.0ms]` tail rendered the full live ancestor chain outermost → innermost with each phase's real wall-clock elapsed time computed from `performance.now()` at snapshot time.
- The elapsed times read 1501.0 ms / 1501.1 ms against the 1500 ms threshold — roughly 1 ms of real-world scheduling jitter, consistent with a healthy Node `setTimeout` on macOS.
- Both the `stuck step=` field AND the `pending=[...]` entries are JSON-quoted, so a future phase name containing a `"` or `\n` character cannot break the single-line parse contract operators will rely on when grepping production stderr. Covered by an explicit vitest case (`escapes embedded newlines and quotes in phase names so the line stays single-line`).
- Direct `process.stderr.write` carried the diagnostic out of the process without routing through `gatewayLog.*`, so a starved or filtered logger pipeline in the real hang case cannot eat the diagnostic — operationally exactly what point 3 of the design spec calls for.

**What was not tested:** A live `openclaw gateway` startup with a real Kubernetes pod and a real induced phase hang under `kubectl logs -f` was not exercised here — the smoke harness above runs the watchdog code path end-to-end in process but does not boot the full gateway. A follow-up real-cluster reproduction (deliberately stall one phase in `server.impl.ts:386` then watch `kubectl logs` for the stderr line) is a sensible next step before this is relied on for production triage, but the runtime behavior of the watchdog itself — timer firing, snapshot accuracy, single-line emit, format correctness — is covered above against the production modules.


---

## ⚠️ Known limitation — post-bind window is intentionally NOT covered

This watchdog disarms at **`http.bound`** (the `startupTrace.mark("http.bound")` point, after **all** bind hosts complete — cancel is fired there in `server.impl.ts`, deliberately *not* in the per-host `onListening` callback in `http-listen.ts`, so a multi-bind hang where the first host binds but `::1` stalls cannot silently disarm the watchdog between binds).

Consequently the watchdog covers the window **`[gateway] starting...` → HTTP `http.bound`** only. **Any hang that occurs *after* the socket has bound — most notably the post-bind plugin/sidecar load path (`loadGatewayStartupPlugins`, the original GH-5814 wedge class) — is invisible to this watchdog by design.** Once `http.bound` fires, the timer is cancelled; a subsequent wedge produces no `[startup-watchdog]` line.

This is an accepted scope boundary for this PR (diagnostic coverage of the *pre-bind* startup phases, which today emit nothing on hang). Extending coverage into the post-bind sidecar-load window is deliberately out of scope here and would require a separate disarm seam (e.g. arming a second watchdog across plugin load, disarmed at first-ready) — tracked separately, not in this change.

## Rebase note (source-line anchors)

This branch has been rebased onto current `upstream/main` (`418d7e1e83`); the `a1f1895b1b`-based "Source-line navigation" table above is therefore **approximate** — absolute line numbers have shifted. On the rebased tree the canonical anchors are: arm at `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts:832` (outside the per-respawn `start:` closure, which opens at `:842`, so it arms once per gateway-loop entry, not per respawn); primary disarm at the `http.bound` mark in `src/gateway/server.impl.ts`; secondary disarm in the `run.ts` startup-failure `catch`. The diff itself is the source of truth for exact placement.
